---
format:
  revealjs:
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-visibility: hidden 
    theme: [default, title-ARCH.scss]
    margin: 0.05
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    navigation-mode: vertical
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    embed-resources: true 
    self-contained: true
    controls: false
mainfont: "Cambria Math"
---

```{=html}
<section class="title-slide">

  <div class="header-band">
    <div class="logo-box">
      <img src="angrau_logo.jpeg" alt="ANGRAU Logo">
    </div>
    <div class="inst-block">
      <div class="university-name">
        Acharya N.G. Ranga Agricultural University
      </div>
      <div class="college-name">
        Agricultural College, Bapatla
      </div>
      <div class="dept-name">
        Department of Statistics and Computer Applications
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="mid-content">
    <div class="course-label">
      AECO 606 ADVANCED AGRICULTURAL MARKETING AND PRICE ANALYSIS
    </div>
    <div class="main-title">
      Autoregressive Conditional Heteroscedasticity
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="footer-band">
    <div class="presenter-block">
      <span class="role-label">Presenter</span>
      <span class="person-name">Krupesh S (BAD/2025-25)</span>
      <span class="person-detail">I Ph.D.(Ag.) Agricultural Statistics</span>
    </div>
  </div>

</section>
```

## Characteristics of Economic Time Series
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| fig-align: "center"
#| out-width: "90%"

library(ggplot2)
library(gridExtra)

set.seed(123)
n <- 2000
df <- data.frame(t = 1:n)

df$p1 <- cumsum(rnorm(n, mean = 0.05, sd = 1))

garch_resid <- rnorm(n)
sigma <- rep(1, n)
for(i in 2:n) {
  sigma[i] <- sqrt(0.1 + 0.85 * sigma[i-1]^2 + 0.1 * garch_resid[i-1]^2)
}
df$p2 <- garch_resid * sigma

df$p3 <- as.numeric(arima.sim(model = list(ar = 0.99), n = n))
df$p4 <- cumsum(rnorm(n, 0, 1))

market_factor <- cumsum(rnorm(n, 0, 1))
df$p5_a <- market_factor + cumsum(rnorm(n, 0, 0.5))
df$p5_b <- market_factor + cumsum(rnorm(n, 0, 0.5)) - 20

jumps <- rnorm(n, 0, 1)
jumps[1200] <- -80
df$p6 <- cumsum(jumps)

my_theme <- theme_classic() +
  theme(
    axis.title       = element_text(size = 9, family = "sans"),
    axis.text        = element_text(size = 7),
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10, family = "sans"),
  )

plot1 <- ggplot(df, aes(x=t, y=p1)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=0.3, color="black") +
  labs(title="Clear Trend (Drift)", x="", y="Price") + my_theme

plot2 <- ggplot(df, aes(x=t, y=p2)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=0.2, color="black") +
  labs(title="Non-constant Volatility (Clustering)", x="", y="Returns") + my_theme

plot3 <- ggplot(df, aes(x=t, y=p3)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=0.3, color="black") +
  labs(title="High Persistence (Long Memory)", x="", y="Deviation") + my_theme

plot4 <- ggplot(df, aes(x=t, y=p4)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=0.3, color="black") +
  labs(title="Meandering (Random Walk)", x="", y="Price") + my_theme

plot5 <- ggplot(df, aes(x=t)) +
  geom_line(aes(y=p5_a), linewidth=0.3, color="black") +
  geom_line(aes(y=p5_b), linewidth=0.3, color="grey50") +
  labs(title="Comovements (Correlation)", x="Time", y="Price") + my_theme

plot6 <- ggplot(df, aes(x=t, y=p6)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=0.3, color="black") +
  labs(title="Structural Break (Jump)", x="Time", y="Price") + my_theme

grid.arrange(plot1, plot2, plot3, plot4, plot5, plot6, ncol = 2)
```

# OBJECTIVE

## Volatility
Volatility means the conditional standard deviation of the underlying price return.

::: {.callout-note}
## Implied they say!
The volatility value that, when plugged into a theoretical pricing model, reproduces the observed market price of an asset is termed as *implied volatility*
:::

## Characteristics of Volatility{.scrollable}

**Unobservability**

- Volatility is *not directly observable* — only one return per trading day exists
- Implied volatility is model-dependent and often overstates true volatility

**Volatility Clustering**

- High volatility periods and low volatility periods tend to cluster together

**Continuous Evolution**

- Volatility changes smoothly over time — abrupt jumps are rare

**Mean Reversion (Stationarity)**

- Volatility does not diverge to $\infty$ — it fluctuates within a bounded range

**Leverage Effect**

- Volatility reacts *asymmetrically* to price increases vs. price drops

## Why Volatility?{style="text-align: justify;"}
The price level reveals the market's current valuation of a commodity, while volatility captures the uncertainty and risk embedded in the price discovery process that led to that equilibrium.

## Homoscedastic vs Heteroscedastic Process
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 16
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-align: "center"
#| out-width: "95%"

library(ggplot2)
library(gridExtra)

set.seed(101)
N <- 500

my_theme <- theme_classic() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10, family = "sans"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 9, family = "sans"),
    axis.text  = element_text(size = 7)
  )

# ── Series A: Homoscedastic ─────────────────────────────────────
# Returns drawn iid Normal — constant variance by construction
r_homo <- rnorm(N, mean = 0.0003, sd = 0.01)
P_homo <- 100 * cumprod(1 + r_homo)

fit_homo   <- arima(r_homo, order = c(1, 0, 0))
resid_homo <- as.numeric(residuals(fit_homo))

# ── Series B: Heteroscedastic ───────────────────────────────────
# Returns with GARCH(1,1) variance — volatility clustering
vt     <- rnorm(N)
h      <- numeric(N)
r_het  <- numeric(N)
h[1]   <- 0.0001

for (t in 2:N) {
  h[t]    <- 0.00001 + 0.15 * r_het[t-1]^2 + 0.80 * h[t-1]
  r_het[t] <- vt[t] * sqrt(h[t])
}

P_het <- 100 * cumprod(1 + r_het)

fit_het   <- arima(r_het, order = c(1, 0, 0))
resid_het <- as.numeric(residuals(fit_het))

# ── Time indices ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
t_idx <- 1:N

# ── Row 1: Price series ─────────────────────────────────────────
p1 <- ggplot(data.frame(t = t_idx, P = P_homo), aes(x = t, y = P)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.4, color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Price Series \u2014 Homoscedastic",
       x = "", y = expression(P[t])) +
  my_theme

p2 <- ggplot(data.frame(t = t_idx, P = P_het), aes(x = t, y = P)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.4, color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Price Series \u2014 Heteroscedastic",
       x = "", y = expression(P[t])) +
  my_theme

# ── Row 2: Returns ───────────────────────────────────────────────
p3 <- ggplot(data.frame(t = t_idx, r = r_homo), aes(x = t, y = r)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.3, color = "grey30") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.3, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "grey60") +
  labs(title = "Returns \u2014 Homoscedastic",
       x = "", y = expression(r[t])) +
  my_theme

p4 <- ggplot(data.frame(t = t_idx, r = r_het), aes(x = t, y = r)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.3, color = "grey30") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.3, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "grey60") +
  labs(title = "Returns \u2014 Heteroscedastic",
       x = "", y = expression(r[t])) +
  my_theme

# ── Row 3: Squared residuals ────────────────────────────────────
p5 <- ggplot(data.frame(t = t_idx, e2 = resid_homo^2), aes(x = t, y = e2)) +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = t, yend = 0), linewidth = 0.3, color = "grey30") +
  labs(title = expression("Squared Residuals " * hat(epsilon)[t]^2 *
                            " \u2014 Homoscedastic"),
       x = "Time", y = expression(hat(epsilon)[t]^2)) +
  my_theme

p6 <- ggplot(data.frame(t = t_idx, e2 = resid_het^2), aes(x = t, y = e2)) +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = t, yend = 0), linewidth = 0.3, color = "grey30") +
  labs(title = expression("Squared Residuals " * hat(epsilon)[t]^2 *
                            " \u2014 Heteroscedastic"),
       x = "Time", y = expression(hat(epsilon)[t]^2)) +
  my_theme

grid.arrange(p1, p2, p3, p4, p5, p6, ncol = 2, nrow = 3)
```


# THE OLS SETUP

## Problem
* Many economic time series exhibit periods of unusually large volatility followed by periods of relative tranquility.
* In such circumstances, the assumption of a constant variance (homoscedasticity) is inappropriate.

## Model
One approach to model this behaviour is to explicitly introduce an independent variable $x_i$ such that,
$$
y_{t+1} = \epsilon_{t+1}x_t
$${#eq-hs}
where,  
$y_{t+1}$ is the dependent variable of interest;  
$\epsilon_{t+1}$ is a white-noise disturbance term with variance $\sigma^2$;  
$x_t$ is an independent variable that can be observed at period $t$.  
Here,
$$
var(y_{t+1}|x_t) = x_t^2\sigma^2
$${#eq-varhs}

## Logarithmic Form
Modifying the @eq-hs, we get,
$$
\ln(y_t) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \ln(x_{t-1}) + e_t
$${#eq-lnform}
where,
$e_t=\ln(\epsilon_t)$

## Regression and its Limitations
Since @eq-lnform is linear, OLS can be used to estimate the parameters $\alpha_0$ and $\alpha_1$.

But,

* The model forces ${x_t}$ to affect mean of $\ln(y_t)$:

  &nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;It is difficult to choose one candidate for ${x_t}$ over many reasonable choices.
    
* Transformations are required for the series to have a constant variance.

# THE ARCH PROCESS

## The "Conditional" Advantage
Consider a stationary ARMA model,
$$y_t = a_0 + a_1y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$$
```{=html}
<table style="width:100%; border-collapse:collapse; font-size:0.85em;">
  <thead>
    <tr style="background-color:#333333; color:white;">
      <th style="border:1px solid #888; padding:10px 14px;text-align:center;">Expectation Type</th>
      <th style="border:1px solid #888; padding:10px 14px;text-align:center;">Mean</th>
      <th style="border:1px solid #888; padding:10px 14px;text-align:center;">Forecast Error Variance</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr style="background-color:#e8e8e8;">
      <td style="border:1px solid #888; padding:10px 14px; font-weight:bold; color:#222222;">Conditional</td>
      <td style="border:1px solid #888; padding:10px 14px;">\(E_t y_{t+1} = a_0 + a_1 y_t\)</td>
      <td style="border:1px solid #888; padding:10px 14px;">\(E_t[(y_{t+1} - a_0 - a_1 y_t)^2] = E_t\epsilon_{t+1}^2 = \sigma^2\)</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background-color:#ffffff;">
      <td style="border:1px solid #888; padding:10px 14px; font-weight:bold; color:#222222;">Unconditional</td>
      <td style="border:1px solid #888; padding:10px 14px;">\(Ey_{t+1} = \dfrac{a_0}{1-a_1}\)</td>
      <td style="border:1px solid #888; padding:10px 14px;">\(E\left[\left(y_{t+1} - \dfrac{a_0}{1-a_1}\right)^2\right] = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{1-a_1^2}\)</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>
```
The conditional forecast uses information from the past and its variance is certainly less than the unconditional counterpart. Thus conditional forecasts are preferable.

## Modelling Non-constant variance
When the conditional variance is not constant, a simple strategy is to model the conditional variance as an AR($q$) process using squares of the estimated residuals:
$$\hat{\epsilon}_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1\hat{\epsilon}_{t-1}^2 + \alpha_2\hat{\epsilon}_{t-2}^2 + \cdots + \alpha_q\hat{\epsilon}_{t-q}^2 + v_t$${#eq-arch}
where $v_t$ is a white-noise process.

If the values of $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, ..., \alpha_n$ all equal to zero, the estimated variance is simply the constant $\alpha_0$.

::: {.callout-note}
## ARCH ALERT
The model in @eq-arch is the stepping stone to the ARCH process
:::

# ENGLE'S ARCH SPECIFICATION

## The Flaw with additive model
* In @eq-arch, $\hat{\epsilon}_{t}^2$ should be strictly non-negative. Also the coefficients are constrained to be non-negative to ensure the expected variance stays positive.
* However, $\nu_t$ is a white-noise process, it can take negative values. If there is a sufficiently large negative shock on $\nu_t$, the entire right hand side of @eq-arch can become mathematically negative.

## The Multiplicative Alternative

In 1982, Robert. F. Engle proposed a class of multiplicative models as:
$$\epsilon_t = v_t \sqrt{\alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2}$${#eq-multARCH}
which is known to be the ARCH($q$) process with $\alpha_0 > 0$, $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum\alpha_i < 1$.

## Deriving the Conditional Variance{.scrollable}

Starting from @eq-multARCH:

$$\epsilon_t = v_t\sqrt{\alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2}$$

Taking $\text{Var}(\cdot \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1})$ on both sides:

$$\text{Var}(\epsilon_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) 
= \left(\alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2\right) 
\cdot \underbrace{\text{Var}(v_t)}_{ = 1}$$

$$\therefore \quad \boxed{\sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2}$${#eq-condvar}

## Compact Form of ARCH($q$)

Substituting $\sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2$ 
back into @eq-multARCH:

$$\epsilon_t = v_t\sqrt{\alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2} 
= v_t\sqrt{\sigma_t^2} = v_t\sigma_t$${#eq-compactARCH}

where $\sigma_t > 0$, since $\alpha_0 > 0$ and $\alpha_i \geq 0$.

## Unconditional Variance of ARCH($q$){.scrollable}
Since $E(\epsilon_t) = 0$, we have $\text{Var}(\epsilon_t) = E(\epsilon_t^2)$. 

Applying the Law of Iterated Expectations:

$$\text{Var}(\epsilon_t) = E\left[E(\epsilon_t^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1})\right] = E(\sigma_t^2) = E\left(\alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2\right)$$

Under stationarity, $E(\epsilon_t^2) = E(\epsilon_{t-i}^2) = \text{Var}(\epsilon_t)$ for all $i$. 

Denoting $\text{Var}(\epsilon_t) = \sigma^2$:

$$\sigma^2 = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \sigma^2 \implies \sigma^2\left(1 - \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i\right) = \alpha_0$$

$$\boxed{\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\alpha_0}{1 - \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i}}$${#eq-uncondvar}

## Mean of the ARCH($q$) Process

From @eq-compactARCH, $\epsilon_t = v_t\sigma_t$, where $\sigma_t$ is $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$-measurable.

**Conditional Mean:**
$$E(\epsilon_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = \sigma_t \cdot E(v_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = \sigma_t \cdot 0 = 0$$

since $v_t \sim \text{iid}(0,1)$ is independent of $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$.

**Unconditional Mean:**
$$E(\epsilon_t) = E\left[E(\epsilon_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1})\right] = E[0] = 0$$

by the **Law of Iterated Expectations**.

## Heavy Tails of the ARCH Process{style="text-align: justify;"}

As long as $\alpha_i \neq 0$ for at least one $i$, $\kappa > 3$, the ARCH($q$) process has  
**heavier tails than the normal distribution**, provided $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^q 3\alpha_i^2 < 1$ where $\kappa$ is the measure of kurtosis.

## Testing for ARCH Effects: Engle's LM Test

$\mathbf{H_0}: \alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \cdots = \alpha_q = 0$ (no ARCH effects)

*Step 1*: Fit an appropriate mean equation (ARMA or OLS regression) and obtain squared residuals $\{\hat{\epsilon}_t^2\}$.

*Step 2*: Regress $\hat{\epsilon}_t^2$ on its $q$ lagged values as in @eq-arch:

$$\hat{\epsilon}_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1\hat{\epsilon}_{t-1}^2 + \alpha_2\hat{\epsilon}_{t-2}^2 + \cdots + \alpha_q\hat{\epsilon}_{t-q}^2 + v_t$$

*Step 3*: Compute the test statistic. Under $H_0$:

$$NR^2 \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2(q)$$
For small samples, an $F$-test with $(q,\ N-q)$ df is preferred.

## Maximum Likelihood Estimation of ARCH($q$){.scrollable}

Given $N$ observations, the parameter vector is $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\alpha_0, \alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_q)^\top$.

**Conditional Log-Likelihood:**

Assuming $v_t \sim N(0,1)$, the conditional density of $\epsilon_t$ given $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$ is:

$$f(\epsilon_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_t^2}}\exp\left(-\frac{\epsilon_t^2}{2\sigma_t^2}\right)$$

The conditional log-likelihood over $t = q+1, \ldots, N$ is:

$$\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{t=q+1}^{N}\left[\log(2\pi) + \log(\sigma_t^2) + \frac{\epsilon_t^2}{\sigma_t^2}\right]$$

## ML Algorithm{.scrollable}

1. Initialize $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(0)}$ — typically via OLS on $\hat{\epsilon}_t^2$ against its lags
2. At iteration $k$, evaluate $\sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2$ recursively for $t = q+1, \ldots, N$
3. Compute $\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)})$
4. Update $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k+1)}$ via **BFGS** quasi-Newton optimization
5. Repeat steps 2--4 until $\|\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k+1)} - \boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)}\| < \delta$

::: {.callout-warning}
## Constraints
Optimization is subject to $\alpha_0 > 0$, $\alpha_i \geq 0$, and $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^q \alpha_i < 1$ to ensure positivity of $\sigma_t^2$ and second-order stationarity.
:::


## Effect of ARCH Coefficients
```{=html}
<div style="font-family: sans-serif; font-size: 13px;">

  <div style="display:flex; align-items:center; gap:16px; margin-bottom:8px;">
    <label style="font-weight:bold; white-space:nowrap;">
      &alpha;<sub>1</sub> + &alpha;<sub>2</sub> =
      <span id="sum_label" style="color:#8c1a1a; font-size:15px;">0.05</span>
    </label>
    <input type="range" id="alpha_slider" min="0" max="100" step="1" value="5"
           style="width:60%; accent-color:#1a1a8c;">
    <span style="font-size:11px; color:#555;">
      &alpha;<sub>1</sub>=<span id="a1_label">—</span>,
      &alpha;<sub>2</sub>=<span id="a2_label">—</span>
    </span>
  </div>

  <div style="display:flex; gap:10px;">
    <canvas id="series_canvas" width="560" height="290"
            style="border:1px solid #ddd; background:#fff;"></canvas>
    <canvas id="density_canvas" width="560" height="290"
            style="border:1px solid #ddd; background:#fff;"></canvas>
  </div>

</div>

<script>
(function () {

  // ── Seeded RNG (Mulberry32) ───────────────────────────────────
  function mulberry32(seed) {
    return function () {
      seed |= 0; seed = seed + 0x6D2B79F5 | 0;
      let t = Math.imul(seed ^ seed >>> 15, 1 | seed);
      t = t + Math.imul(t ^ t >>> 7, 61 | t) ^ t;
      return ((t ^ t >>> 14) >>> 0) / 4294967296;
    };
  }

  function makeNormal(rng) {
    // Box-Muller
    const u = rng(), v = rng();
    return Math.sqrt(-2 * Math.log(u)) * Math.cos(2 * Math.PI * v);
  }

  const N = 300;
  const rng = mulberry32(42);
  const vt = Array.from({ length: N }, () => makeNormal(rng));

  // ── ARCH(2) simulation ────────────────────────────────────────
  function simARCH2(a1, a2) {
    const eps = new Array(N).fill(0);
    for (let t = 2; t < N; t++) {
      const sig2 = Math.max(1 + a1 * eps[t-1]**2 + a2 * eps[t-2]**2, 1e-6);
      eps[t] = vt[t] * Math.sqrt(sig2);
    }
    return eps;
  }

  // ── KDE ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  function kde(data, xs, bw) {
    return xs.map(x => {
      const s = data.reduce((acc, xi) => {
        const u = (x - xi) / bw;
        return acc + Math.exp(-0.5 * u * u);
      }, 0);
      return s / (data.length * bw * Math.sqrt(2 * Math.PI));
    });
  }

  function normalPDF(xs, mu, sd) {
    return xs.map(x => {
      const u = (x - mu) / sd;
      return Math.exp(-0.5 * u * u) / (sd * Math.sqrt(2 * Math.PI));
    });
  }

  function stdDev(arr) {
    const m = arr.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0) / arr.length;
    return Math.sqrt(arr.reduce((a, b) => a + (b - m) ** 2, 0) / arr.length);
  }

  // ── Canvas helpers ────────────────────────────────────────────
  const PAD = { l: 48, r: 15, t: 42, b: 32 };

  function clearAndAxes(ctx, W, H, xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax,
                         xlabel, ylabel, title, subtitle) {
    const pw = W - PAD.l - PAD.r;
    const ph = H - PAD.t - PAD.b;
    ctx.clearRect(0, 0, W, H);

    ctx.fillStyle = '#111';
    ctx.font = 'bold 12px sans-serif';
    ctx.textAlign = 'left';
    ctx.fillText(title, PAD.l, 16);

    ctx.fillStyle = '#666';
    ctx.font = '10px sans-serif';
    ctx.fillText(subtitle, PAD.l, 30);

    ctx.strokeStyle = '#777';
    ctx.lineWidth = 0.8;
    ctx.beginPath();
    ctx.moveTo(PAD.l, PAD.t);
    ctx.lineTo(PAD.l, PAD.t + ph);
    ctx.lineTo(PAD.l + pw, PAD.t + ph);
    ctx.stroke();

    // y ticks
    ctx.fillStyle = '#555';
    ctx.font = '9px sans-serif';
    for (let i = 0; i <= 4; i++) {
      const v  = ymin + (ymax - ymin) * i / 4;
      const py = PAD.t + ph - (v - ymin) / (ymax - ymin) * ph;
      ctx.textAlign = 'right';
      ctx.fillText(v.toFixed(1), PAD.l - 4, py + 3);
      ctx.strokeStyle = '#eee';
      ctx.lineWidth = 0.4;
      ctx.beginPath();
      ctx.moveTo(PAD.l + 1, py);
      ctx.lineTo(PAD.l + pw, py);
      ctx.stroke();
    }

    // x ticks
    ctx.textAlign = 'center';
    for (let i = 0; i <= 5; i++) {
      const v  = xmin + (xmax - xmin) * i / 5;
      const px = PAD.l + (v - xmin) / (xmax - xmin) * pw;
      ctx.fillStyle = '#555';
      ctx.fillText(v.toFixed(0), px, PAD.t + ph + 14);
    }

    // axis labels
    ctx.fillStyle = '#333';
    ctx.font = '10px sans-serif';
    ctx.textAlign = 'center';
    ctx.fillText(xlabel, PAD.l + pw / 2, H - 2);
    ctx.save();
    ctx.translate(12, PAD.t + ph / 2);
    ctx.rotate(-Math.PI / 2);
    ctx.fillText(ylabel, 0, 0);
    ctx.restore();

    return { pw, ph };
  }

  function px(v, vmin, vmax, size, offset) {
    return offset + (v - vmin) / (vmax - vmin) * size;
  }

  function py(v, vmin, vmax, size, offset) {
    return offset + size - (v - vmin) / (vmax - vmin) * size;
  }

  // ── Draw series ───────────────────────────────────────────────
  function drawSeries(eps, sa) {
    const cv  = document.getElementById('series_canvas');
    const ctx = cv.getContext('2d');
    const W = cv.width, H = cv.height;
    const ymin = Math.min(...eps) * 1.15;
    const ymax = Math.max(...eps) * 1.15;

    const { pw, ph } = clearAndAxes(
      ctx, W, H, 0, N, ymin, ymax,
      'Time', '\u03B5(t)',
      'ARCH(2) Process',
      '\u03B1\u2081 + \u03B1\u2082 = ' + sa.toFixed(2)
    );

    // zero line
    const zy = py(0, ymin, ymax, ph, PAD.t);
    ctx.strokeStyle = '#ccc'; ctx.lineWidth = 0.6;
    ctx.setLineDash([4, 4]);
    ctx.beginPath();
    ctx.moveTo(PAD.l, zy); ctx.lineTo(PAD.l + pw, zy);
    ctx.stroke(); ctx.setLineDash([]);

    ctx.strokeStyle = '#1a1a8c'; ctx.lineWidth = 0.9;
    ctx.beginPath();
    eps.forEach((v, i) => {
      const x = px(i, 0, N, pw, PAD.l);
      const y = py(v, ymin, ymax, ph, PAD.t);
      i === 0 ? ctx.moveTo(x, y) : ctx.lineTo(x, y);
    });
    ctx.stroke();
  }

  // ── Draw density ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  function drawDensity(eps, sa) {
    const cv  = document.getElementById('density_canvas');
    const ctx = cv.getContext('2d');
    const W = cv.width, H = cv.height;

    const xs   = Array.from({ length: 200 }, (_, i) => -10 + i * 0.1);
    const sd   = stdDev(eps);
    const bw   = 1.06 * sd * Math.pow(eps.length, -0.2);
    const arch = kde(eps, xs, bw);
    const norm = normalPDF(xs, 0, sd);
    const ymax = Math.max(...arch, ...norm) * 1.2;

    const { pw, ph } = clearAndAxes(
      ctx, W, H, -10, 10, 0, ymax,
      '\u03B5(t)', 'Density',
      'Tail Behaviour vs Normal',
      '\u03B1\u2081 + \u03B1\u2082 = ' + sa.toFixed(2)
    );

    function curve(ys, color, dash) {
      ctx.strokeStyle = color;
      ctx.lineWidth = 1.4;
      ctx.setLineDash(dash || []);
      ctx.beginPath();
      xs.forEach((x, i) => {
        const cx = px(x,    -10, 10,   pw, PAD.l);
        const cy = py(ys[i], 0,  ymax, ph, PAD.t);
        i === 0 ? ctx.moveTo(cx, cy) : ctx.lineTo(cx, cy);
      });
      ctx.stroke();
      ctx.setLineDash([]);
    }

    curve(norm, '#555555', [6, 4]);
    curve(arch, '#8c1a1a');

    // legend
    const lx = PAD.l + pw - 140, ly = PAD.t + 8;
    ctx.lineWidth = 2;
    ctx.strokeStyle = '#8c1a1a'; ctx.setLineDash([]);
    ctx.beginPath(); ctx.moveTo(lx, ly); ctx.lineTo(lx+22, ly); ctx.stroke();
    ctx.fillStyle = '#333'; ctx.font = '10px sans-serif'; ctx.textAlign = 'left';
    ctx.fillText('ARCH(2)', lx + 26, ly + 4);

    ctx.strokeStyle = '#555555'; ctx.setLineDash([6, 4]);
    ctx.beginPath(); ctx.moveTo(lx, ly+16); ctx.lineTo(lx+22, ly+16); ctx.stroke();
    ctx.setLineDash([]);
    ctx.fillText('Normal', lx + 26, ly + 20);
  }

  // ── Slider logic ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  function update() {
    const sa = +document.getElementById('alpha_slider').value / 100;
    const a1 = +(sa * 0.6).toFixed(3);
    const a2 = +(sa * 0.4).toFixed(3);
    document.getElementById('sum_label').textContent = sa.toFixed(2);
    document.getElementById('a1_label').textContent  = a1;
    document.getElementById('a2_label').textContent  = a2;
    const eps = simARCH2(a1, a2);
    drawSeries(eps, sa);
    drawDensity(eps, sa);
  }

  document.getElementById('alpha_slider').addEventListener('input', update);
  update();

})();
</script>
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Alpha dynamics
As $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$, volatility clusters intensify and tails grow
heavier — the ARCH process departs increasingly from normality.
:::

## Building an ARCH Model{.scrollable}

1. **Compute Log Returns** — Transform raw price series $P_t$ to log returns $r_t = \ln(P_t/P_{t-1})$. This ensures stationarity in mean, removes trend, and stabilises scale.

2. **Fit a Mean Equation** — Fit an appropriate ARMA($p$,$q$) model on $r_t$ using AIC/BIC. Extract residuals $\hat{\epsilon}_t$.

3. **Test for ARCH Effects** — Run Engle's LM test on $\hat{\epsilon}_t$. Reject $H_0$ to confirm volatility clustering warrants ARCH modelling.

4. **Identify Order $q$** — Examine PACF of $\hat{\epsilon}_t^2$. The lag at which PACF cuts off suggests candidate $q$.

5. **Estimate ARCH($q$)** — Maximise conditional log-likelihood via BFGS. Verify $\alpha_0 > 0$, $\alpha_i \geq 0$, and $\sum\alpha_i < 1$.

6. **Diagnose Residuals** — Standardise residuals as $\tilde{\epsilon}_t = \hat{\epsilon}_t/\hat{\sigma}_t$. Check:
    - ACF of $\tilde{\epsilon}_t$ and $\tilde{\epsilon}_t^2$ for remaining structure
    - Ljung-Box test on $\tilde{\epsilon}_t^2$
    - QQ-plot for distributional assumption

7. **Iterate if Needed** — If $\tilde{\epsilon}_t^2$ still shows significant ACF, increase $q$ or consider GARCH.

8. **Forecast** — Use fitted $\hat{\sigma}_t^2$ for volatility forecasting and interval construction.

# EMPIRICAL ILLUSTRATION
## Data: Deutsch Mark / British Pound Exchange Rate Returns
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 16
#| fig-height: 5

library(rugarch)
library(FinTS)
library(ggplot2)
library(gridExtra)
library(forecast)
library(tseries)

data("dmbp", package = "rugarch")
r <- dmbp[, 1]
n <- length(r)
t <- 1:n
df <- data.frame(t = t, r = r)

my_theme <- theme_classic() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.text  = element_text(size = 7)
  )

p1 <- ggplot(df, aes(x = t, y = r)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.4, color = "black") +
  labs(title = "DM/BP Exchange Rate Returns",
       x = "Time", y = "Returns") +
  my_theme

p2 <- ggplot(df, aes(x = r)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 50, fill = "grey60",
                 color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  stat_function(fun = dnorm,
                args = list(mean = mean(r), sd = sd(r)),
                color = "black", linewidth = 0.7,
                linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Distribution vs Normal",
       x = "Returns", y = "Density") +
  my_theme

# Squared returns as volatility proxy
p3 <- ggplot(df, aes(x = t, y = r^2)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.4, color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Squared Returns (Volatility Proxy)",
       x = "Time", y = expression(r[t]^2)) +
  my_theme

grid.arrange(p1, p2, p3, ncol = 3)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Data
Deutsch Mark / British Pound exchange rate returns.  
$N = `r n`$ observations. Canonical dataset for ARCH demonstration.
:::

## Step 1: Log Returns {auto-animate=true}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "1-4"

library(rugarch)
data("dmbp", package = "rugarch")
r <- dmbp[, 1]
head(r)
```
```{r}
#| echo: false
head(r)
```

- Series is already in log return form: $r_t = \ln(P_t / P_{t-1})$
- Mean close to zero, exhibits volatility clustering visually

## Step 2: Stationarity Test {auto-animate=true}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "1-2"

library(tseries)
adf.test(r)
```
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
adf.test(r)
```

- ADF test strongly rejects $H_0$ of unit root
- Series is stationary — no differencing required

## Step 3: Fit Candidate Mean Equations {.scrollable auto-animate=true}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "1-17"

models <- list(
  "AR(1)"     = arima(r, order = c(1,0,0)),
  "AR(2)"     = arima(r, order = c(2,0,0)),
  "MA(1)"     = arima(r, order = c(0,0,1)),
  "MA(2)"     = arima(r, order = c(0,0,2)),
  "ARMA(1,1)" = arima(r, order = c(1,0,1)),
  "ARMA(1,2)" = arima(r, order = c(1,0,2)),
  "ARMA(2,1)" = arima(r, order = c(2,0,1)),
  "ARMA(2,2)" = arima(r, order = c(2,0,2))
)
ic_tbl <- data.frame(
  Model = names(models),
  AIC   = round(sapply(models, AIC), 3),
  BIC   = round(sapply(models, BIC), 3)
)
ic_tbl
```
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

models <- list(
  "AR(1)"     = arima(r, order = c(1,0,0)),
  "AR(2)"     = arima(r, order = c(2,0,0)),
  "MA(1)"     = arima(r, order = c(0,0,1)),
  "MA(2)"     = arima(r, order = c(0,0,2)),
  "ARMA(1,1)" = arima(r, order = c(1,0,1)),
  "ARMA(1,2)" = arima(r, order = c(1,0,2)),
  "ARMA(2,1)" = arima(r, order = c(2,0,1)),
  "ARMA(2,2)" = arima(r, order = c(2,0,2))
)
ic_tbl <- data.frame(
  Model = names(models),
  AIC   = round(sapply(models, AIC), 3),
  BIC   = round(sapply(models, BIC), 3)
)
print(ic_tbl, row.names = FALSE)
```
```{r}
#| echo: false
best_name <- ic_tbl$Model[which.min(ic_tbl$BIC)]
mean_fit  <- models[[best_name]]
p_order   <- mean_fit$arma[1]
q_order   <- mean_fit$arma[2]
resid     <- residuals(mean_fit)
```

- Best mean equation by BIC: **`r best_name`**
- Residuals $\hat{\epsilon}_t$ extracted for ARCH testing

## Step 4: ACF and PACF of Squared Residuals {auto-animate=true}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "1-6"

acf_r2  <- acf(resid^2,  lag.max = 12, plot = FALSE)
pacf_r2 <- pacf(resid^2, lag.max = 12, plot = FALSE)
ci      <- qnorm(0.975) / sqrt(length(resid))

```
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 3.6
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-align: center
#| warning: false

acf_r2  <- acf(resid^2,  lag.max = 12, plot = FALSE)
pacf_r2 <- pacf(resid^2, lag.max = 12, plot = FALSE)
ci      <- qnorm(0.975) / sqrt(length(resid))

df_acf <- data.frame(
  lag  = as.numeric(acf_r2$lag[-1]),
  val  = as.numeric(acf_r2$acf[-1]),
  type = "ACF of Squared Residuals"
)
df_pacf <- data.frame(
  lag  = as.numeric(pacf_r2$lag),
  val  = as.numeric(pacf_r2$acf),
  type = "PACF of Squared Residuals"
)
df_all <- rbind(df_acf, df_pacf)

# Identify significant PACF lags for annotation
sig_pacf <- which(abs(pacf_r2$acf) > ci)
max_lag  <- 5 

ggplot(df_all, aes(x = lag, y = val)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0,
             color = "black", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = lag, yend = 0),
               linewidth = 0.7, color = "grey30") +
  geom_point(aes(fill = abs(val) > ci),
             shape = 21, size = 2,
             color = "black") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "white",
                                "TRUE"  = "black"),
                    guide  = "none") +
  geom_hline(yintercept =  ci, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -ci, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ type, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:12) +
  labs(x = "Lag", y = "Value") +
  my_theme +
  theme(
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
    strip.background = element_blank(),
    panel.spacing    = unit(1.5, "lines")
  )
```

- Filled points exceed the 95% confidence band — significant lags
- PACF cuts off at lag **`r max_lag`** — suggests **ARCH(`r max_lag`)**

## Step 5: Engle's LM Test {auto-animate=true}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "1-2"

library(FinTS)
ArchTest(resid, lags = max_lag)
```
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
ArchTest(resid, lags = max_lag)
```

- $p$-value $< 0.05$ — formally confirms ARCH effects
- Maximum lag set to **`r max_lag`** from PACF inspection

## Step 6: Fit and Compare ARCH Orders {.scrollable auto-animate=true}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "1-20"

arch_fit <- function(q) {
  spec <- ugarchspec(
    variance.model     = list(model      = "sGARCH",
                               garchOrder = c(q, 0)),
    mean.model         = list(armaOrder    = c(p_order, q_order),
                               include.mean = TRUE),
    distribution.model = "norm"
  )
  ugarchfit(spec, data = r, solver = "hybrid")
}

q_max  <- max(max_lag, 2)
fits   <- lapply(1:q_max, arch_fit)
ic_mat <- sapply(fits, infocriteria)

ic_tbl <- data.frame(
  Order = paste0("ARCH(", 1:q_max, ")"),
  AIC   = round(ic_mat[1, ], 5),
  BIC   = round(ic_mat[2, ], 5)
)
ic_tbl
```
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Redefine explicitly to avoid scope issues
best_name <- ic_tbl$Model[which.min(ic_tbl$BIC)]
mean_fit  <- models[[best_name]]
p_order   <- mean_fit$arma[1]
q_order   <- mean_fit$arma[2]

arch_fit <- function(q) {
  spec <- ugarchspec(
    variance.model     = list(model      = "sGARCH",
                               garchOrder = c(q, 0)),
    mean.model         = list(armaOrder    = c(p_order, q_order),
                               include.mean = TRUE),
    distribution.model = "norm"
  )
  tryCatch(
    ugarchfit(spec, data = r, solver = "hybrid"),
    error = function(e) NULL
  )
}

q_max  <- 5
fits   <- lapply(1:q_max, arch_fit)
fits   <- Filter(Negate(is.null), fits)
ic_mat <- sapply(fits, infocriteria)

ic_tbl2 <- data.frame(
  Order = paste0("ARCH(", seq_along(fits), ")"),
  AIC   = round(ic_mat[1, ], 5),
  BIC   = round(ic_mat[2, ], 5)
)
print(ic_tbl2, row.names = FALSE)
```
```{r}
#| echo: false
best_q   <- which.min(ic_mat[2, ])
best_fit <- fits[[best_q]]
```

- Best model by BIC: **ARCH(`r best_q`)**
- BIC preferred for stronger parsimony penalty

## Step 7: Best Model Estimates {.scrollable auto-animate=true}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "1"

coef(best_fit)
```
```{r}
#| echo: false
round(coef(best_fit), 5)
```
```{r}
#| echo: false
cf <- coef(best_fit)
a0 <- round(cf["omega"], 5)
sa <- round(sum(cf[grep("alpha", names(cf))]), 5)
```

- $\hat{\alpha}_0 = `r a0`$ — baseline conditional variance
- $\sum\hat{\alpha}_i = `r sa` < 1$ — **stationarity condition satisfied**

## Step 8: Residual Diagnostics {.scrollable auto-animate=true}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "1-4"

std_resid <- residuals(best_fit, standardize = TRUE)
ArchTest(std_resid,   lags = best_q)
Box.test(std_resid,   lag  = best_q, type = "Ljung-Box")
Box.test(std_resid^2, lag  = best_q, type = "Ljung-Box")
```
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
std_resid <- residuals(best_fit, standardize = TRUE)
ArchTest(std_resid,   lags = best_q)
Box.test(std_resid,   lag  = best_q, type = "Ljung-Box")
Box.test(std_resid^2, lag  = best_q, type = "Ljung-Box")
```

- LM test on $\tilde{\epsilon}_t$: $p > 0.05$ — **no remaining ARCH effects**
- Ljung-Box on $\tilde{\epsilon}_t$: $p > 0.05$ — **no linear autocorrelation**
- Ljung-Box on $\tilde{\epsilon}_t^2$: $p > 0.05$ — **white noise confirmed**

## Step 8: QQ-Plot {auto-animate=true}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "1-6"

ggplot(data.frame(r = as.numeric(std_resid)),
       aes(sample = r)) +
  stat_qq(size = 0.8, alpha = 0.6) +
  stat_qq_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "QQ-Plot: Standardised Residuals") +
  theme_classic()
```
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 3.2
#| fig-width: 5
#| fig-align: center

ggplot(data.frame(r = as.numeric(std_resid)),
       aes(sample = r)) +
  stat_qq(size = 0.6, alpha = 0.5, color = "grey30") +
  stat_qq_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "QQ-Plot: Standardised Residuals",
       x = "Theoretical Quantiles",
       y = "Sample Quantiles") +
  my_theme
```

- Mild tail departure — Student-$t$ innovations may improve fit

## Step 9: Volatility Forecast {auto-animate=true}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "1-2"

forc <- ugarchforecast(best_fit, n.ahead = 20)
plot(forc, which = 3)
```
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 3.2
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-align: center
#| warning: false

forc    <- ugarchforecast(best_fit, n.ahead = 20)
sig_f   <- as.numeric(sigma(forc))
sig_fit <- as.numeric(sigma(best_fit))
n_fit   <- length(sig_fit)

df_forc <- data.frame(
  t     = c(1:n_fit, (n_fit+1):(n_fit+20)),
  sigma = c(sig_fit, sig_f),
  type  = c(rep("Fitted", n_fit), rep("Forecast", 20))
)

ggplot(df_forc, aes(x = t, y = sigma, color = type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Fitted"   = "grey60",
                                "Forecast" = "black")) +
  labs(title = "Conditional Volatility: Fitted + 20-step Forecast",
       x = "Time",
       y = expression(hat(sigma)[t]),
       color = "") +
  my_theme +
  theme(legend.position = "top")
```

- Forecast converges toward unconditional volatility
- Confirms mean-reverting nature of the fitted ARCH process

# TIMELINE OF ARCH MODEL DEVELOPMENT{.scrollable}

```{=html}
<div style="font-family: sans-serif; font-size: 0.78em; padding: 0 10px;">
<table style="width:100%; border-collapse:collapse;">
  <thead>
    <tr style="background:#333; color:#fff;">
      <th style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:center; width:10%;">Year</th>
      <th style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:left;  width:22%;">Model</th>
      <th style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:left;  width:28%;">Author(s)</th>
      <th style="padding:8px 12px; text-align:left;  width:40%;">Key Contribution</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr style="background:#f0f0f0;">
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:bold;">1982</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; font-weight:bold;">ARCH(<i>q</i>)</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Engle</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Time-varying conditional variance driven by past squared shocks</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#ffffff;">
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:bold;">1986</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; font-weight:bold;">GARCH(<i>p,q</i>)</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Bollerslev</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Adds lagged variance terms — parsimonious long-memory volatility</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#f0f0f0;">
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:bold;">1987</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; font-weight:bold;">ARCH-M</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Engle, Lilien &amp; Robins</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Volatility enters the mean equation — formalises risk-return tradeoff</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#ffffff;">
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:bold;">1988</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; font-weight:bold;">IGARCH</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Engle &amp; Bollerslev</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Integrated GARCH — shocks to variance are permanent</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#f0f0f0;">
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:bold;">1991</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; font-weight:bold;">EGARCH</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Nelson</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Exponential form — asymmetric leverage effect, no sign constraints</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#ffffff;">
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:bold;">1991</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; font-weight:bold;">Semi-parametric ARCH</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Engle &amp; González-Rivera</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Non-parametric innovation distribution — relaxes normality assumption</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#f0f0f0;">
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:bold;">1992</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; font-weight:bold;">GJR-GARCH</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Glosten, Jagannathan &amp; Runkle</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Threshold asymmetry — separates impact of positive and negative shocks</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#ffffff;">
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:bold;">1992</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; font-weight:bold;">News Impact Curve</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Engle &amp; Ng</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Measures asymmetric impact of positive vs negative news on &sigma;&#x0302;<sub>t</sub><sup>2</sup></td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#f0f0f0;">
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:bold;">1992</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; font-weight:bold;">Implied ARCH</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Engle &amp; Chowdhury</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Extracts volatility dynamics from options prices — links ARCH with options markets</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#ffffff;">
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:bold;">1992</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; font-weight:bold;">MGARCH / FACTOR-ARCH</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Bollerslev &nbsp;|&nbsp; Engle, Ng &amp; Rothschild</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Multivariate conditional covariance — factor structure for large portfolios</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#f0f0f0;">
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; text-align:center; font-weight:bold;">1993</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px; font-weight:bold;">Stochastic Volatility</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Harvey, Ruiz &amp; Shephard</td>
      <td style="padding:7px 12px;">Latent volatility process — alternative paradigm to GARCH framework</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>
</div>
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Nobel Recognition
Robert F. Engle was awarded the **Nobel Prize in Economics in 2003** for the ARCH framework and its role in modern financial econometrics.
:::

# WEAKNESSES OF ARCH MODELS

1. **Symmetric Response** — Uses $\epsilon_{t-i}^2$, so positive and negative shocks 
have identical effects on volatility. Financial assets respond asymmetrically in practice.

2. **Restrictive Constraints** — For ARCH(1), $\alpha_1^2 \leq \dfrac{1}{3}$ is required 
for finite kurtosis. Constraints become increasingly complex at higher orders.

3. **No Causal Insight** — Mechanically describes conditional variance behaviour 
but gives no indication of what drives volatility clustering.

4. **Overpredicts Volatility** — Responds slowly to large isolated shocks, 
causing persistent overestimation of volatility after outlier events.


# THE ARCH-M MODIFICATION

## Motivation{.scrollable}

Risk-averse agents demand compensation for holding a volatile asset — the risk premium itself should rise with conditional variance.

**The Three-Equation System** (Engle, Lilien & Robins, 1987):

$$y_t = \mu_t + \varepsilon_t \qquad \text{(Mean equation)}$${#eq-archm-mean}

$$\mu_t = \beta + \delta h_t, \quad \delta > 0 \qquad \text{(Risk premium)}$${#eq-archm-rp}

$$h_t = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \varepsilon_{t-i}^2 \qquad \text{(Variance equation)}$${#eq-archm-var}

where $y_t$ is the excess return, $\mu_t$ is the time-varying risk premium, and $h_t$ is the ARCH($q$) conditional variance.

::: {.callout-note}
## Key Interpretations
- $\delta > 0$ — higher conditional variance raises expected return (risk–return tradeoff)  
- $\delta = 0$ — ARCH-M degenerates to standard ARCH; risk premium becomes constant $\beta$  
- $\delta$ is estimated jointly with $\alpha_i$ via MLE — its significance is the central test
:::

## How it differs from ARCH(q)

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 16
#| fig-height: 7

library(ggplot2)
library(gridExtra)

# ── Parameters ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
set.seed(42)
N      <- 300
alpha0 <- 0.5
alpha1 <- 0.6

# ── Innovations ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
vt    <- rnorm(N)
eps   <- numeric(N)
h     <- numeric(N)
h[1]  <- alpha0 / (1 - alpha1)

for (t in 2:N) {
  h[t]   <- alpha0 + alpha1 * eps[t-1]^2
  eps[t] <- vt[t] * sqrt(h[t])
}

# ── Processes ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
beta0   <- -0.3
delta   <- 0.8
y_arch  <- beta0 + eps
y_archm <- beta0 + delta * h + eps

# ── Data frames ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
t_idx <- 1:N

df_arch  <- data.frame(t = t_idx, y = y_arch)
df_archm <- data.frame(t = t_idx, y = y_archm)

# Squared residuals: eps^2 as volatility proxy
# For ARCH:  eps_t = y_t - beta0
# For ARCH-M: eps_t = y_t - beta0 - delta*h_t  →  same eps either way
df_vol_arch <- data.frame(
  t    = t_idx,
  eps2 = eps^2,
  panel = "Squared Residuals — ARCH(1):  same \u03b5\u209c drives both"
)
df_vol_archm <- data.frame(
  t    = t_idx,
  eps2 = eps^2,
  panel = "Squared Residuals — ARCH-M(1): \u03b4\u00b7h\u209c shifts mean at these spikes"
)
df_vol <- rbind(df_vol_arch, df_vol_archm)

my_theme <- theme_classic() +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 8, color = "grey30"),
    axis.title    = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.text     = element_text(size = 7),
    strip.text    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 8),
    strip.background = element_blank()
  )

# ── Row 1: ARCH series ────────────────────────────────────────────
p1 <- ggplot(df_arch, aes(x = t, y = y)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.3,
             color = "grey60", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.4, color = "black") +
  labs(
    title    = "ARCH(1)",
    subtitle = expression(y[t] == beta[0] + epsilon[t] ~~ "(mean stays flat)"),
    x = "", y = expression(y[t])
  ) +
  my_theme

# ── Row 1: ARCH-M series ──────────────────────────────────────────
p2 <- ggplot(df_archm, aes(x = t, y = y)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.3,
             color = "grey60", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.4, color = "black") +
  labs(
    title    = "ARCH-M(1)",
    subtitle = expression(y[t] == beta[0] + delta~h[t] + epsilon[t] ~~ "(mean rises with variance)"),
    x = "", y = expression(y[t])
  ) +
  my_theme

# ── Row 2: Squared residuals — two facets side by side ────────────
p3 <- ggplot(df_vol_arch, aes(x = t, y = eps2)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.3,
             color = "grey60", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.35, color = "grey30") +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.25, fill = "grey40") +
  labs(
    title    = expression(epsilon[t]^2 ~ "(Volatility Proxy)"),
    subtitle = "Spikes here → flat mean in ARCH above",
    x = "Time", y = expression(epsilon[t]^2)
  ) +
  my_theme

p4 <- ggplot(df_vol_archm, aes(x = t, y = eps2)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.3,
             color = "grey60", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.35, color = "grey30") +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.25, fill = "grey40") +
  labs(
    title    = expression(epsilon[t]^2 ~ "(Volatility Proxy)"),
    subtitle = expression("Spikes here \u2192 " * delta~h[t] * " lifts mean in ARCH-M above"),
    x = "Time", y = expression(epsilon[t]^2)
  ) +
  my_theme

# ── Arrange: 2x2 grid ─────────────────────────────────────────────
grid.arrange(p1, p2, p3, p4, ncol = 2, nrow = 2)
```

# ARCH FAMILY
## ARCH Family Explorer
```{=html}
<iframe src="arch-family-explorer-v5.html"
        width="1080px" height="620px"
        style="border:none; display:block; margin:0 auto;">
</iframe>
```


# REFERENCES

## 
```{=html}
<div style="font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 2.2; font-family: 'Cambria Math', serif;">

<p style="padding-left: 2em; text-indent: -2em;">
1. Engle, R. F. (1982). Autoregressive conditional heteroscedasticity with estimates of the variance of United Kingdom inflation. <em>Econometrica, 50</em>(4), 987–1007.
</p>

<p style="padding-left: 2em; text-indent: -2em;">
2. Engle, R. F., Lilien, D. M., &amp; Robins, R. P. (1987). Estimating time varying risk premia in the term structure: The ARCH-M model. <em>Econometrica, 55</em>(2), 391–407.
</p>

<p style="padding-left: 2em; text-indent: -2em;">
3. Enders, W. (2015). <em>Applied econometric time series</em> (4th ed.). John Wiley &amp; Sons.
</p>

<p style="padding-left: 2em; text-indent: -2em;">
4. Tsay, R. S. (2010). <em>Analysis of financial time series</em> (3rd ed.). John Wiley &amp; Sons.
</p>

<p style="padding-left: 2em; text-indent: -2em;">
5. Ghalanos, A. (2025). <em>rugarch: Univariate GARCH models</em> (R package version 1.5-4). 
</p>

</div>
```
